questions.question_text: Under Delaware law, where must a newly licensed salesperson conduct real estate activities?
distractors: Independently after passing the exam
Under any experienced salesperson
Through the Real Estate Commission directly
questions.explanation: Delaware requires all salespersons to work under the supervision of a licensed broker. A salesperson cannot conduct real estate activities independently, regardless of experience or exam passage. The broker is responsible for overseeing the salesperson's conduct.

questions.question_text: Maria holds an associate broker license in Delaware. She decides to work at ABC Realty under Broker Kim. What is Maria's status at the firm?
questions.correct_answer: Associate broker supervised by Broker Kim
distractors: Co-broker sharing equal authority with Kim
Independent contractor with no oversight
Temporary broker until she opens her firm
questions.explanation: In Delaware, an associate broker holds broker qualifications but chooses to work under another broker rather than operate independently. Maria is still supervised by Broker Kim and must follow the firm's policies. Holding broker qualifications does not exempt her from oversight.

questions.question_text: A Delaware buyer wants to pay a bonus directly to the salesperson who found her dream home. How must this compensation be handled?
questions.correct_answer: It must be paid through the broker
distractors: The buyer may pay the salesperson directly
The salesperson files it with the Commission
Direct payment is fine if under $500
questions.explanation: Delaware law requires all compensation to flow through the broker. A salesperson cannot accept payment directly from any party to a transaction. The buyer must pay the broker, who then distributes compensation to the salesperson according to their agreement.

questions.question_text: A salesperson at XYZ Realty makes misleading statements to a buyer during a showing. Under Delaware law, when may the salesperson's broker also face discipline for this conduct?
questions.correct_answer: If the broker knew of the conduct or failed to supervise
distractors: Always, because brokers are strictly liable for licensee acts
Never, because each licensee answers only for personal acts
Only if the buyer first wins a civil lawsuit
questions.explanation: The salesperson is directly subject to discipline for misrepresentation under 24 Del. C. §2912(a)(1). The broker is responsible for day-to-day supervision of the firm (§2902), and a supervision failure can itself support discipline, but §2912(b) provides that a broker's license may not be revoked for another licensee's unlawful act unless the Commission finds the broker had knowledge of it.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes the broker's supervisory obligation under Delaware real estate law?
questions.correct_answer: The broker must supervise all firm licensees
distractors: Supervision applies only to new salespersons
Brokers supervise salespersons but not associates
Supervision is optional for experienced agents
questions.explanation: Delaware law requires the broker to supervise all licensees within the firm, regardless of their experience level or license type. This includes salespersons and associate brokers alike. A branch office must have a designated on-site supervisor with at least five years' experience, but the branch, its licensees, and the on-site supervisor all remain under the broker's direction and supervision (24 Del. C. §§2902(a)(11), 2919(d)).

questions.question_text: After closing a residential sale in Delaware, a broker must retain copies of all transaction documents for what purpose?
questions.correct_answer: To comply with Commission retention rules
distractors: To provide certified copies to the assessor
To submit quarterly reports to the state
To satisfy federal tax reporting mandates
questions.explanation: Delaware brokers must keep, in the office or electronically available there, a complete record of all moneys received or escrowed on real estate transactions, retained for at least 3 years (Commission Rule 6.3), and all books, records, and written agreements must be accessible for Commission inspection (24 Del. C. §2923(e)). This is a state licensing requirement, not a federal tax obligation.

questions.question_text: A Delaware broker completes a transaction and places all signed documents in storage. Three months later, a Commission investigator requests to review them. What must the broker do?
distractors: Decline until a court order is obtained
Provide only the settlement statement
Refer the investigator to the title company
questions.explanation: Delaware brokers must keep escrow-related books, records, and written agreements accessible at the approved place of business, open to Commission inspection or audit during regular business hours (24 Del. C. §2923(e)). No court order is required, and refusing or producing only partial records can result in disciplinary action.

questions.question_text: A Delaware salesperson negotiates a purchase agreement and both parties sign. When must executed copies of the agreement be delivered to the parties?
questions.correct_answer: Promptly after execution by all parties
distractors: Within 30 days of the closing date
Only after the inspection period expires
At the final settlement meeting only
questions.explanation: Commission Rule 8.4.1 requires that each party receive a copy of the agreement when they sign or initial it, and a fully executed copy within a reasonable time after all parties have signed. Copies may be paper or electronic. Waiting until closing or the end of a contingency period is not acceptable.

questions.question_text: A Delaware broker keeps transaction files at a remote storage facility. A licensee audit reveals several files are missing key documents. What is the most likely consequence?
distractors: A warning with no further action taken
Automatic license suspension for one year
Mandatory reimbursement to all past clients
questions.explanation: Brokers are required to maintain copies of all transaction documents and make them available for Commission inspection. Missing documents represent a failure to meet this obligation and can result in disciplinary action, which may include fines, license suspension, or other penalties depending on the severity. Note that escrow and money records specifically must be kept in the broker's office or be electronically available there (Rule 6.3), and escrow-related records must be accessible at the approved place of business for Commission inspection (§2923(e)).

questions.question_text: Which documents must a Delaware broker retain copies of after a completed transaction?
questions.correct_answer: All documents related to the transaction
distractors: Only the listing agreement and deed
Only documents signed by the broker
Only the settlement statement and contract
questions.explanation: Delaware requires brokers to keep all books, records, written agreements, and other necessary documents accessible for Commission inspection (24 Del. C. §2923(e)), and complete money/escrow records for at least 3 years (Rule 6.3). This includes listing agreements, purchase contracts, addenda, disclosures, and settlement statements. Keeping only selected documents, such as the listing agreement and deed, does not satisfy these obligations.

questions.question_text: A Delaware broker receives a $10,000 earnest money deposit. Where must these funds be placed?
questions.correct_answer: In a designated trust or escrow account
distractors: In the broker's personal checking account
In the firm's general operating account
In a savings account under the buyer's name
questions.explanation: Delaware law requires that earnest money and other client funds be deposited into a designated trust or escrow account. Placing client funds in a personal or general business account constitutes commingling, which is a serious violation of Delaware real estate law.

questions.question_text: Broker Davis deposits a client's $5,000 earnest money check into his personal business account to "keep better track of it." What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Commingling of client and personal funds
distractors: Conversion of funds for personal benefit
Failure to endorse the check properly
Unauthorized practice of banking services
questions.explanation: Mixing client funds with personal or business operating funds is commingling, which is prohibited under Delaware law. Even if the broker intends to keep the funds safe, they must be held in a separate trust or escrow account. Commingling can result in serious disciplinary consequences.

questions.question_text: A Delaware broker keeps $150 of personal funds in the trust account to cover monthly bank service fees. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, small amounts for bank fees are allowed
distractors: No, any personal funds constitute commingling
Only if the Commission gives written approval
Only if every affected client consents first
questions.explanation: Delaware permits a broker to maintain a small amount of personal funds in the trust account solely to cover bank service charges. This prevents the account from being depleted by fees and is an exception to the general prohibition against commingling. The amount must be reasonable and limited to covering bank costs.

questions.question_text: The Delaware Real Estate Commission suspects a broker is mishandling client funds. What authority does the Commission have regarding the trust account?
questions.correct_answer: The Commission may audit at any time
distractors: Audits require a court order first
The Commission must give 60 days notice
Only the state attorney general can audit
questions.explanation: The Commission and its authorized agents may inspect or audit a broker's escrow accounts and records without a court order or advance-notice requirement; the statute provides for inspection at the broker's approved place of business during regular business hours (24 Del. C. §2923(e)). This power ensures that client funds are being properly maintained and that brokers are complying with trust account regulations.

questions.question_text: A purchase contract in Delaware states earnest money will be held by the listing broker. The buyer's agent deposits the funds with a title company instead. What issue does this create?
questions.correct_answer: Violation of the contract's deposit terms
distractors: No issue since title companies are licensed
A minor paperwork error easily corrected
An automatic voiding of the contract
questions.explanation: Earnest money must be handled according to the terms of the purchase contract. If the contract specifies the listing broker will hold the deposit, sending it elsewhere violates those terms. While title companies can hold escrow funds, the parties agreed to specific terms that must be followed.
